step_by_step_reasoning: The whiteboard depicts two distinct user roles (ADMIN and USERS), a load balancer, an Amazon ECR registry, an Amazon EKS cluster running the Nginx Kubernetes Ingress Controller (KIC), and three backend service pods (A, B, C). Based on this: 1. ADMIN (UserCompanyDeveloper) pushes containers to ECR and deploys via kubectl to EKS. 2. USERS (UserConsumerWeb) access the services via the Load Balancer (ELB). 3. The Load Balancer forwards traffic to the Nginx Ingress Controller on EKS, which performs routing, security, and authentication before distributing traffic to backend service pods (EKS).

graph.name: Nginx Ingress Controller on Amazon EKS
graph.notes: Architecture illustrating modern application delivery using Nginx Kubernetes Ingress Controller (KIC) on Amazon EKS to manage, secure, and route traffic to backend microservices.

nodes.name: Administrator
nodes.notes: Uses Docker and kubectl to manage configurations and deploy applications.

nodes.name: End Users
nodes.notes: Initiate traffic to access the web applications.

nodes.name: Elastic Load Balancing
nodes.notes: Provides entry-point high-availability infrastructure load balancing.

nodes.name: Amazon Elastic Container Registry
nodes.notes: Stores Docker container images built by the administrator.

nodes.name: Nginx Ingress Controller (KIC)
nodes.notes: Runs on EKS. Handles URL routing, TLS termination, WAF (App Protect), and authentication.

nodes.name: Backend Services (A, B, C)
nodes.notes: Target application pods running inside EKS.

edges.notes: Pushes built Docker images to ECR.
Applies configurations and deployments using kubectl.
Pulls container images to run on EKS.
Accesses the application via standard web protocols.
Forwards edge traffic to the Ingress Controller.
Performs Layer 7 routing and forwards traffic to services A, B, or C.
